text: Water bath canning is used for high-acid foods like fruits, tomatoes, pickles, and jams. The boiling water temperature of 212°F (100°C) is sufficient to destroy molds, yeasts, and some bacteria in these acidic foods. Process jars for the time specified in tested recipes, adjusting for altitude. At altitudes above 1,000 feet, increase processing time by 5 minutes for every 5,000 feet.
source: USDA Complete Guide to Home Canning

text: Pressure canning is the only safe method for canning low-acid foods including vegetables, meats, poultry, and seafood. These foods must be processed at 240°F (116°C), which can only be reached with a pressure canner operating at 10-15 PSI. A boiling water canner cannot reach this temperature. Failure to use pressure canning for low-acid foods risks botulism, a potentially fatal foodborne illness caused by Clostridium botulinum toxin.
source: USDA Complete Guide to Home Canning

text: To can tomatoes safely, add acid to each jar: 2 tablespoons of bottled lemon juice per quart, or 1 tablespoon per pint. Alternatively, add 1/2 teaspoon of citric acid per quart or 1/4 teaspoon per pint. This ensures the pH is below 4.6, the threshold for safe water bath canning. Do not use fresh lemon juice as its acidity varies.
source: USDA Complete Guide to Home Canning

text: Dehydrating food removes moisture to prevent bacterial growth. Most fruits and vegetables should be dried to 10-20% moisture content. Use a food dehydrator set to 125-135°F (52-57°C) for fruits and 130-140°F (54-60°C) for vegetables. Slice foods uniformly to 1/4 inch thickness for even drying. Pre-treat light-colored fruits with ascorbic acid solution to prevent browning.
source: USDA Complete Guide to Home Canning

text: Salt-curing meat is one of the oldest preservation methods. For a basic dry cure, use 8 pounds of salt per 100 pounds of meat. Rub the salt mixture thoroughly into all surfaces, paying special attention to areas around bones. Store at 36-40°F (2-4°C) during the curing period of 7 days per inch of meat thickness. After curing, soak the meat for 30 minutes per pound to remove excess salt before smoking or cooking.

Lacto-fermentation preserves vegetables using beneficial Lactobacillus bacteria. Submerge vegetables in a 3-5% salt brine (1-2 tablespoons salt per quart of water). Use non-iodized salt — iodine inhibits beneficial bacteria. Keep vegetables fully submerged below the brine using a weight. Ferment at 68-75°F (20-24°C) for 1-4 weeks depending on taste preference. Store finished ferments in the refrigerator or a root cellar at 32-40°F.

Root cellaring stores produce using the earth's natural cool temperature and humidity. An ideal root cellar maintains 32-40°F (0-4°C) with 85-95% humidity. Root vegetables (carrots, beets, turnips, potatoes) store 4-6 months under these conditions. Apples store 2-4 months. Keep apples separate from other produce as they release ethylene gas which accelerates ripening. Ensure adequate ventilation to prevent mold — at minimum two vents, one at floor level and one at ceiling.

Freezing is the simplest preservation method. Blanch vegetables before freezing to stop enzyme action that causes loss of flavor, color, and texture. Blanching times vary: corn on cob 7-11 minutes, green beans 3 minutes, broccoli 3 minutes, peas 1.5 minutes. Immediately plunge into ice water after blanching for the same duration. Pack in moisture-vapor resistant containers leaving 1/2 inch headspace for expansion. Frozen vegetables maintain quality for 8-12 months at 0°F (-18°C).
source: USDA Complete Guide to Home Canning
